news.Title: Apple at 50: how Asia fuelled its rise to the top
news.AI_Summary: Japanese industry and Chinese manufacturing prowess played roles in the tech giant’s success, with consequences for both
news.Content:
Our collective memory is wrong. The story of Apple’s comeback from the brink of bankruptcy in the late 1990s to the birth of the iPhone in 2007 is almost always told as the triumphant tale of Steve Jobs’ product vision coupled with the clever designs of Jony Ive.

But the triumphant years were also manufacturing hell, as Apple synthesised competing systems of how to build products at scale, quickly, and without defects. “It was trial by fire,” says Tony Fadell, who rose from contractor to senior vice-president in this period. “We didn’t know what we didn’t know.”

Apple was battling one crisis after another, from the stock collapse of the 2000 dotcom crash to notable product failures like the G4 Cube. Despite the cultural success of the iMac and the iPods, profitability was so low that in Jobs’ first seven years as chief executive, Apple achieved a cumulative net profit of around $2.1bn. Today, Apple earns $2.1bn of profit per week.

For the relatively small group of people travelling the globe to get the infrastructure up and running, these years could be painful and not everyone lasted the course. “All that ‘insanely great’ design had a hell of a body count,” says David Lundgren, manager of product design for the first iMac.

But what Apple discovered in these years was profound: it learnt to separate knowing how from doing. The lessons took years to master, as the Apple teams developed quality systems with elements inherited from rivals, bridged them with cutting-edge designs, and did it all at breakneck speed. It was only with the iPod nano in 2005 that Apple began to sync it all together, laying the foundation for the most important manoeuvre the company would ever make — entering the phone market.

That move would turn Apple into the world’s most valuable company by the time of Jobs’ death in 2011. But over the next decade Apple’s success would have even bigger consequences for Japanese industry and China’s manufacturing prowess, as companies in Asia’s two largest economies experienced mirror-image trajectories based on how they fitted into the emerging Apple empire.

In a statement, Apple took issue with this account of its rise. “The entire premise of this story is false and doesn’t reflect how we work with our supply chain partners,” it said.

In the early 1990s, when Apple’s portfolio expanded from a few products per year to dozens, the company had developed a quality management framework called ANPP, or Apple New Product Process, a concept-to-customer roadmap for building products. As soon as Jobs returned in 1997, it proved inadequate.

“Jobs wanted a real shallow organisation,” says a former manufacturing engineer. “There was this incredible bureaucracy built up with all these vice-presidents and all these different groups — all these different silos, basically. Nobody was talking to each other.” Jobs conducted a mass clear-out at the VP level, the person adds, “except for Jackie Streeter.”

This is the second in a two-part series looking at the origins of the philosophy that powered the company’s rise

Part one: The legacy of an unsung visionary in a fallen empirePart two: The influence of Japanese craft and Chinese hard graft

Streeter had joined Apple in the mid-1980s and, as head of central engineering, had co-developed ANPP. That Jobs kept her in place is significant. The younger Jobs had declared it was “better to be a pirate than join the Navy”. This high-risk approach had built the Mac, an era-defining success, but it had also built the Apple Lisa, the Apple III, and the NeXTcube — all commercial flops. The older Steve had grasped the importance of process, the unglamorous world of ensuring quality and consistency.

But as Apple conjured up the “Bondi Blue”-coloured iMac and sought to have it in stores 14 months later, the ANPP proved outdated. “People thought we were crazy,” recalls Jon Rubinstein, then the senior vice-president of hardware engineering. “We basically replaced the ANPP. We tore it all up.”

Starting anew brought major challenges. Six months into the iMac project Jobs learned that the engineers couldn’t build it as designed, forcing a project reset. He went on a tirade, yelling at the project’s senior leaders: “Ninety-five fucking hundred jobs are depending on you, and you’ve failed!”

Rubinstein and Glen Miranker, senior engineers who had each joined Apple after working at Motorola, introduced more rigour. They adapted a three-phase quality framework to carry the iMac through engineering, development and production.

“It’s kaizen — continuous improvement,” Rubinstein says, referring to the Japanese business philosophy. “I had a whole team working on the process by which we develop products.”

Apple’s framework, still known today as ANPP despite all the changes, was refined further with the iPod. In 2001, Apple took just nine months from conceiving the product to shipping it, a dazzling feat reflecting a new cross-functional approach.

Fadell says quality checks at every manufacturing station proved to be a lifeline. A 1 per cent defect rate felt acceptable when iPod volumes were shipping in the hundreds of thousands, in 2002, but it would be “a ton of waste” when demand swelled to 50mn units just five years later. “We had one or two production lines, then it became 10, and 20, and 100 assembly lines, all running in parallel,” he says. “If you were making crap, it piled up fast.”

But at the same time, Apple sought to find a balance between reliability and punctuality. New hires hailing from IBM imported a “schedule is king” mentality, eliminating waste with Toyota-inspired “lean management”. Upon joining Apple to lead operations in 1998, Tim Cook emphasised time as a competitive moat.

For some, slowness might confer craftsmanship, but for Cook it was a defect-prone process in disguise. Slowness was time squandered, because time is money. As Cook, who once called himself “Attila the Hun of inventory”, would later explain to someone in the product design team: “Do you understand that every minute is one million dollars lost?”

As Apple shipped products in greater volumes, its partnerships across the supply chain became more important and, critically, more numerous. A breakthrough occurred, albeit one with questionable ethics.

In the first years of Jobs’ comeback, Apple preferred relying on single suppliers for important components. The idea was that if you spread risk by relying on three vendors, one is exceedingly likely to be better than the other two.

That would force the company to widen the engineering constraints to “the lowest common denominator”, says Rubinstein. Jobs, focused on quality, would rather choose the best supplier, and then do whatever was needed to help it succeed.

This was against standard industry practice, but standard industry has the luxury of using off-the-shelf parts made by multiple vendors. Apple, by contrast, constantly pushes suppliers to come up with parts, materials and functions that don’t exist yet. “If we found a company that could already do exactly what we wanted, we weren’t doing the right thing,” says a former engineer.

Yet the sheer scale of Apple’s success forced it to change tack. Relying on single suppliers was madness once iPod volumes reached the tens of millions. To get around “the lowest common denominator” problem Apple would identify the best supplier for a component, then actively teach its rivals the secrets of their manufacturing process until no differences between them remained. Suppliers at the time compared the tactic to “corporate espionage”.

The iPod Classic became a case study. It had a beautiful stainless steel back, 0.4 millimetres thin, and reflective like a mirror thanks to precision hand polishing. Apple had worked with a master craftsman in Japan to develop the process and, within a few years, more than 20 companies were working in co-operation on 15,000 to 20,000 iPod casings per day.

Once the iPod ballooned in popularity, these artisans couldn’t keep up. That’s when the film crews showed up. Apple recorded every step of the process — the angle of the craftsman’s wrist, the pressure and speed and sound of the polishing, and how to manage the heat distortion without warping the metal. Then Apple broke down each step into mathematical code and automated it all, at gargantuan scale, in China.

The episode is detailed in a Japanese book from 2013, The True Face of the Apple Empire, by journalists Naoyoshi Goto and Jun Morikawa.

This tactic was repeated again and again: Ive’s industrial design group would find a Japanese supplier and talk up design, the significance of their work, and promise lots of business. But when operations, the division tasked with scaling at minimal cost, would inherit the relationship, they often found the Japanese to be expensive and risk-averse. Result: Apple would copy the craftsmanship, reverse-engineer it, and scale it elsewhere.

Sharp was another victim. In the late 2000s its LCD TV business was struggling from an abrupt price war with Korean rivals. Apple came calling, needing tens of millions of LCD panels as iPhone growth turned exponential. Apple offered Sharp help to retool its television factory for smartphones, prepaying for future panels with an investment worth nearly $1bn.

Goto, one of the Japanese journalists, was surprised to learn that within the Sharp factory there was a secretive office space for up to 50 people from Apple to work. No Sharp employees were allowed.

But these weren’t Californians; they were from Asia — “display professionals recruited by Apple from Samsung, Motorola, and Taiwanese companies”, he says. “And by signing an exclusive supply contract, Apple gained access to everything: Sharp’s production line knowhow, manufacturing methods, and cost structure.”

Through capital investment, embedding their own people, and ruthless contracts, Apple was in effect controlling the LCD supply chain from the inside. When Apple needed LCDs, utilisation expanded beyond 90 per cent; when it didn’t, the factory turned idle. Apple had paid for the machinery and Sharp wasn’t allowed to supply, say, Samsung. Sharp’s management, Goto says, felt they ate “a poisoned apple”. They had lost control of their own production line.

Apple’s tactics could easily feel like an abuse of power, as four Japanese academics concluded in a 2021 article labelling the practice a “captive form” of governance. For suppliers, these manufacturing secrets were not Apple’s information to share.

Legally, though, Apple had made certain its actions were sound. Its standard purchase order terms require suppliers to assign to Apple, “without reservation, all ownership rights, title, and interest”. Using the carrot of a major iPhone order, Cupertino could routinely exert enormous leverage to own the IP — even if its suppliers did most of the work and R&D funding.

“If we wanted to bring up another supplier, legally, from our standpoint, we would say, ‘Hey, we own the IP’,” says a former manufacturing design engineer. “We can go to someone else and tell them everything they need to know to make it.”

Japan, owing to its niche specialities but relatively high wages, found itself particularly vulnerable.

Apple had found a sideways method to outcompete them. Not by building better products itself, but by taking intellectual ownership of the key processes, then spreading it around. Apple would even install sophisticated lasers to measure every dimension and every colour of its suppliers’ parts, with Cupertino receiving a pipeline feed of the data. Apple took the lessons of Homer Sarasohn’s statistical quality controls, then supercharged them with 21st-century technology.

“The Japanese are still pissed over it,” says a senior supply chain executive. “Their value, their workmanship, their secret sauce, has been completely extracted . . . [Apple would] take the videos and there was nothing you could do about it. They’d call it their IP.”

In the quest to keep innovating and maintain its premium share of the market, Apple built out a team of so-called subject matter experts (SME).

Their job was to scour the globe and find obscure methods behind fancy cameras and watches, then adapt the techniques for making iPhones and MacBooks. Apple job postings confirm that these experts travel to factories 25 per cent of their time, working hands-on to “develop manufacturing processes to scale with our suppliers”.

An analysis of patents co-invented by these experts reveals their seemingly incredible breadth. Matt Theobald, who spent 17 years in Apple hardware, is named as the co-inventor of a “glass alignment for high temperature processes”, a “portable computer unified top case”, a “shed-forming device for a weaving machine”, and a “Jacquard machine” comprised of knives moving in opposite directions.

This range of inventions attributed to the same engineer reflects an Apple playbook: fund supplier capacity, gain operational control, absorb process knowledge by embedding engineers in the factory, contractually take ownership of the IP, then commoditise the supplier by bringing up its rivals.

“I was able to go to Japan, or fly wherever — go to the ends of the earth to work with the coolest suppliers,” says a former SME. “Then we’d figure out how to scale it.”

China and Taiwan, this person says, were great at building “good-enough machines”, whereas Japanese vendors often made machines that were “too good” — in that they couldn’t scale quickly enough to please Apple’s volume-focused operations team. Some former SMEs admit feeling bad about the practice. “I felt horrible about it,” says one. “We’d screw over the original IP owner. I did this over and over.”

Apple insiders say it was not their fault that Japan lost bigger orders. “It’s survival of the fittest,” says a former Apple engineer. “If you have a great idea but can’t deliver it [at scale], you haven’t evolved.”

This person adds: “It was never like, ‘We want to crush the Japanese’ or anything like that. It was all about hardcore business requirements. Like, ‘We need 7mn of these a week in order for us to do this, or it’s not going to work.’”

The playbook Apple mastered over the past quarter century offered a new kind of competitive advantage, one hidden from view but quietly instrumental for its profit margins, ability to innovate and capacity for scale.

It found the best suppliers, extracted their manufacturing DNA, and then codified it into patents, process specifications, and equipment configurations. Apple learnt to understand manufacturing processes so deeply that it could play suppliers off against each other, transfer knowledge between them, and replace any one of them.

It was an enormous paradigm shift. Yet over the same period, China entered the picture with another way of working that threatens not only Apple but the west.

Chinese businessmen have a three-word phrase for it: yang-tao-sha, or “raise-entrap-slaughter”. The idea dates back to the late 1970s, when Deng Xiaoping visited factories in Singapore and Japan and witnessed just how backward China was.

The country was then poorer than sub-Saharan Africa. But what it had in large numbers was people — densely packed, earning low wages. These workers were unskilled, but willing to learn and work hard, and their activities could be governed by a forceful state that could actively direct where they would be used.

What China needed was foreign capital and expertise, plus a way to keep people from leaving for Hong Kong. Instead of erecting a physical barrier it created economic opportunity: Shenzhen was born. A special economic zone where entrepreneurs and local politicians could bring in investment and expertise from overseas.

At the same time, China enacted policies to suppress the costs of exports, keeping wages competitive and its currency undervalued, while granting tax exemptions and subsidising machinery.

Collectively, these acted as a magnet for foreign capital and knowhow, creating the conditions for China’s people to absorb reams of “tacit knowledge” or skills that can only be learnt by doing, a major ingredient in China’s ability to challenge the west in technology and industrial output.

What Apple and China learnt together was that the same workforce stitching garments could be trained to assemble iPods and iPhones. Western corporations systematically devalued the role of manufacturing and focused on product design, branding and finance. This is the basis of the so-called “smiling curve” of product development — with high-value concept development at one end, higher-value sales and branding at the other, and low-value manufacturing at the bottom of the curve.

Apple mastered this so well that it’s now worth $3.7tn and churns out annual profits in excess of $100bn a year. But China has been making a bet that this value curve is, at the national level, inverted: call it “the grumpy curve of product development”.

An individual company like Apple focuses on the ends of the curve to increase profits, but China has embraced the belly of the curve to absorb capabilities. In the long run, China is betting that knowhow and scaled capacity have greater value than profits, which might be ephemeral.

“Longer-term, big macro view, I think the Chinese stepped back and said, ‘Well, if we’re making it all, that’s power,’” says a former Apple operations manager.

The sort of knowledge leaking that had helped Apple learn from suppliers in Japan became a threat in China. So, beginning in 2006, Apple had Foxconn build it a dedicated campus, known as “Apple Garden”, in Guanlan. Within a few years two more emerged, in Chengdu and Zhengzhou.

Today, Apple produces a fifth of the world’s smartphones. But it has no capacity to produce them without China. Meanwhile, Chinese brands hold 50 per cent smartphone market share, plus strategic chokepoints in lithium-ion batteries, rare earth metals, printed circuit boards, and more.

Apple found ways to outcompete the Japanese at a company level, but China recognised another strategic node: where all the building takes place matters. No longer just a low-value assembler, China is emerging as the world’s innovation hub, too.

The trajectory of BYD illustrates the point. BYD began supplying Apple around 2008, making metal casings. It has since become one of Apple’s most important assemblers of the iPad and a supplier for titanium alloy components. That training has helped BYD to build an electric vehicle company that outsells Tesla — reflecting the value of the “grumpy curve”.

In other words Apple did not merely outsource manufacturing to China, it played a starring role in building up its industrial capacity. The contrast with Japan is stark. Over the past quarter century Apple has extracted critical knowhow from Japan’s best minds, turning the country’s famous electronics brands, like Sharp and Sony, into invisible subcontractors inside Apple products. In China, invisible subcontractors, such as BYD, scaled the value chain and evolved into China’s world-leading brands.

Apple’s 50th anniversary this week marks a triumph of design, software and marketing. But the supply chain it built may prove to be its most consequential product — a supply chain it no longer fully controls. It took America a generation to relearn the lessons it had taught Japan. It might not get a second chance this time.

Data visualisation by Alan Smith. Cartography by Cleve Jones

news.Title: Judge halts construction of Donald Trump’s $400mn White House ballroom
news.AI_Summary: First major setback for 90,000 sq ft white and gold project since East Wing was demolished in October
news.Content:
A US federal judge has ordered Donald Trump to stop building a new $400mn White House ballroom until Congress approves its completion.

The decision from district court judge Richard Leon, a George W Bush appointee, is the first setback for the president’s 90,000 sq ft white and gold project since Trump demolished the East Wing in October.

In a 35-page opinion, Leon sided with the National Trust for Historic Preservation in granting the plaintiff’s motion for a preliminary injunction “because no statute comes close to giving the president the authority he claims to have”.

“The President of the United States is the steward of the White House for future generations of first families,” Leon wrote. “He is not, however, the owner!”

“Unless and until Congress blesses this project through statutory authorisation, construction has to stop!”

The order will take effect in 14 days so as to give the National Park Service time to appeal against the decision.

The National Trust is also suing the White House over its plans to renovate the John F Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts, alleging it has failed to comply with environmental laws and historic preservation norms.

Trump announced the ballroom last summer and said private donations would fund the $200mn project. The reported price has since doubled, as the number of seats has soared from 650 to roughly 1,000.

Donations have come from several tech, tobacco, defence and crypto companies, including Google, Meta, Alphabet, Reynolds American, Altria, Lockheed Martin, Tether, Coinbase, Ripple and Gemini.

In a Truth Social post on Tuesday, Trump disparaged the National Trust, writing that the ballroom would be “ahead of schedule”, built “at no cost to the taxpayer” and “be the finest Building of its kind anywhere in the World”.

He added it did not make sense why the private non-profit group did not instead sue the Federal Reserve, led by Jay Powell, for going “billions” over budget for the institution’s renovation.

The president attacked the judge’s ruling in a second post later on Tuesday: “Congressional approval has never been given on anything, in these circumstances, big or small, having to do with construction at the White House. In this case, even less so, because the Ballroom is being built with Private Donations, no Federal Taxpayer Money!”

The appropriations clause of the constitution declares that all money the federal government spends must be appropriated by Congress.

news.Title: OpenAI raises $3bn from retail investors as part of record funding haul
news.AI_Summary: ChatGPT maker pulls in up to $122bn, setting it on path to IPO  as early as this year
news.Content:
OpenAI has raised more than $3bn from retail investors as part of a huge funding round that the company hopes will help it outmuscle rivals, including Anthropic, and set it on the path to an initial public offering as early as this year.

The ChatGPT maker has closed a record funding round of up to $122bn which dwarfs any previous private raise and values the company at $852bn, including the new money.

As part of that deal, OpenAI has tapped retail investors for the first time, through a trio of banks and exchange traded funds managed by Cathie Wood’s ARK Invest.

The company framed their participation as a way of “giving more people the opportunity to share in the upside economics of OpenAI and the AI era”. The deals were the largest private placements the banks had completed, said Sarah Friar, OpenAI’s chief financial officer.

Retail investors are expected to play an important role in the big public listings anticipated over the next 12 months, accounting for as much as 30 per cent of the SpaceX float and participating in IPOs for OpenAI and Anthropic, said people familiar with the matter.

Friar said broadening access was consistent with the company’s mission of ensuring powerful AI was created “for the benefit of humanity.” That meant “not just access to the technology but access to the financial upside”, she said on Tuesday.

Currently, only wealthy retail investors could access the stock, but Friar said one of her main priorities was to expand that in time.

Growing retail exposure to lossmaking private companies is also likely to be a concern. Individual investors tend to be less sophisticated than financial groups such as venture capitalists and are often afforded fewer protections than earlier backers. They tend to gain access later and at higher valuations too.

Retail investors became a mainstay of private capital over the past decade but have begun pulling money from some funds this year amid a downturn in that market.

OpenAI’s partners SoftBank, Amazon and Nvidia committed $110bn of the total raised; Microsoft and venture capital firms including Andreessen Horowitz, Sequoia Capital and Thrive Capital accounted for billions of dollars more.

Amazon’s $50bn commitment is split between a $15bn upfront investment and a $35bn tranche, which will follow when OpenAI either goes public or achieves so-called artificial general intelligence, a breakthrough that Friar defined as “the majority of economically valuable human work being able to be done by [AI] agents”.

OpenAI is raising the new capital to fuel its competition with Anthropic, Google and others building frontier AI models. The company is fighting to build upon and monetise its 900mn or so individual users while also making inroads on selling to businesses.

The San Francisco-based group has jettisoned a number of projects in recent weeks, including its video app Sora and a planned erotic chatbot, as it seeks to focus only on its core business.

OpenAI said it was generating $2bn a month in revenue, roughly 60 per cent from its consumer business and the remainder from enterprises.

news.Title: Private capital’s great disappointment era begins
news.AI_Summary: The $22tn industry is facing the prospect that returns will fall far short of the expectations of investors
news.Content:
One scoop to start: A broker for Pete Hegseth, the US defence secretary, attempted to make a big investment in major defence companies in the weeks leading up to the US-Israeli attack on Iran, according to three people familiar with the matter.

And another: London-based start-up Fractile is in talks to raise more than $200mn as part of a growing group of UK chip companies seeking to challenge Nvidia’s dominance.

And another thing: Unilever is nearing a deal to combine its food business with spice and sauce maker McCormick, which would bring Hellmann’s mayonnaise and French’s mustard under one portfolio.

Welcome to Due Diligence, your briefing on dealmaking, private equity and corporate finance. This article is an on-site version of the newsletter. Premium subscribers can sign up here to get the newsletter delivered every Tuesday to Friday. Standard subscribers can upgrade to Premium here, or explore all FT newsletters. Get in touch with us anytime: [email protected]

In today’s newsletter:

Private capital comes down to earth

Apollo plans a second HQ

Emirates gets a discount

On Monday the Trump administration proposed a rule to open up the $10tn American retirement marketplace to private capital.

Donald Trump’s efforts to give about 90mn US savers access to unlisted assets, first scooped by the FT last year, would unlock for private capital groups an enormous source of untapped funds.

There’s no rule against offering private investments in the retirement accounts, called 401ks, but managers of the plans have historically been reluctant to do so for fear of being sued.

The new rule would offer administrators of retirement plans a “process-based safe harbour” to protect them against litigation, so long as they consider factors to safeguard investors.

The proposal comes at a fraught moment for private markets, with even top White House officials warning about the risks of $4tn in unsold private equity assets.

Last month, US Treasury secretary Scott Bessent warned about “rotten” private equity entering the retirement accounts of ordinary Americans. Still, Bessent signed off on Monday’s ruling.

Private capital bosses argue their funds are well-suited for retirees and have increasingly pitched the “diversification” offered by their funds, instead of eye-watering returns.

But there are doubts about whether private markets are worth higher costs and lower liquidity.

In a Big Read, DD’s Antoine Gara and Eric Platt took a sweeping look at the growing challenges private equity and private credit deals face as more muted returns may yield a great disappointment for investors.

Private capital has been in the limelight in recent months as new AI tools have generated fears that highly leveraged software companies, the epicentre of a boom in dealmaking the past decade, will cause losses among PE buyers and many credit funds that financed the deals. Wealthy “retail” investors that poured into private credit funds have recently raced to redeem their money.

The sector’s problems go back further, with industry-wide returns plummeting since central banks started raising interest rates in 2021, making it difficult for private equity funds to exit investments.

The financial health of private equity-owned companies has also been squeezed by higher rates and geopolitical turmoil. A record percentage of such groups have chosen to increase their debt rather than making their interest payments in cash, Fitch recently found.

On the debt side, ageing deals have proved problematic for lenders, which have slashed the value of many of their holdings. Loans left in debt funds often are of lower quality since stronger businesses have been easily able to refinance in recent years.

The falling returns have caused a flood of redemption requests at funds sold to wealthy investors, presaging the risks that private capital groups will be exposed to as their investor bases change.

As megafirms such as Blackstone, Apollo and KKR increasingly covet money from retirement savers, they have opened their firms, once small shops of buccaneering takeover artists with little regulatory oversight, to far greater scrutiny.

It was appointment television in 2010. In The Decision, basketball superstar LeBron James revealed in a televised interview he would be joining the Miami Heat.

His line, “I’m going to take my talents to South Beach,” may soon be repeated by some of Wall Street’s most feared dealmakers. Otherwise, Texas may want to get ready for some city slickers.

Apollo Global Management — a firm filled with Hamptons aficionados — is looking to add a second headquarters after Manhattan, with Texas or Florida set to eventually win the sweepstakes, the FT scooped on Sunday.

Apollo told the FT that “New York does not have a monopoly on talent” and that the second HQ would be the place where “most of our future growth will take place”.

Not long ago, Apollo was a small, elite firm of leveraged buyout artists housed at 9 West 57th Street.

But with total assets today pushing $1tn and an employee count at more than 4,000, more space is clearly needed.

Florida and Texas have more conservative politics, and with a democratic socialist mayor in New York whom Apollo chief Marc Rowan has called an “enemy”, one can see the political and culture war signalling in the move.

New York has at least scored recent big commitments from JPMorgan Chase, American Express and Bank of America.

Whether it’s private credit evangelising or junior associate recruiting, Apollo has leaned into being a Wall Street tastemaker.

Expect other top-tier firms to kick around the idea of finding a cheaper and friendlier place to store some employees.

But DD wonders how many rainmakers would actually give up life in the big city for highways and strip malls.

Airlines flying into the Middle East are facing insurance rates so high that one executive at a European carrier likened the quotes they had received to “blackmail”.

But one airline has secured “war risk” insurance for a steal.

Emirates, the international airline known for offering ritzy first class travel, is paying an order of magnitude less in insurance than rivals to land its fleet in Dubai, the FT scoops.

The Dubai-based airline’s broker WTW has negotiated a policy in which it pays an additional war-risk premium of about $100,000 a week to cover its planes landing in and taking off from Dubai.

That looks cheap compared with other airlines that are being quoted $70,000 to $150,000 in additional charges for every flight that lands in the region.

One insurance executive said Emirates’ rate looked “outrageously” low given the heightened risk of transit to and from the Middle East, where the past four weeks of conflict have caused thousands of flights to be cancelled.

Gulf airlines do benefit from the experience of operating hundreds of flights a day through the region’s airspace, as well as close co-ordination with its airports and authorities, industry executives told the FT.

Still, at least one rival airline insurer declined to sign up for Emirates’ policy, which is led by Atrium.

“It’s very cheap cover, that we and others have not agreed to,” said one executive with knowledge of the policy.

But another insurer — who did agree to provide cover at the cheaper rate — said the carrier’s size and market power made it hard to say no: “You effectively have to fall in line, otherwise you risk not writing Emirates”.

Standard Chartered has hired Ole Matthiessen as global head, transaction services and digital assets in Singapore. He previously worked at Deutsche Bank.

Squire Patton Boggs has named Andrew Wilkinson as European managing partner, effective in May.

BP head of electric vehicle charging Martin Thomsen has departed to join Rolls-Royce as chief procurement and supply chain officer.

Xerox has appointed Louie Pastor as chief executive. He was most recently the photocopier-maker’s chief operating officer.

Air Canada chief executive Michael Rousseau will step down later this year after provoking a political uproar over his failure to speak French in a video message addressing a fatal New York plane crash.

Lending practices Bangladesh’s former land minister Saifuzzaman Chowdhury has been Market Financial Solutions’ most-scrutinised borrower. But the collapsed UK mortgage provider also lent to a number of others accused of financial crimes, the FT reports.

Condiment boss The new CEO of Kraft Heinz has overseen the break-up of a business before, but he says this group should stay whole. Steve Cahillane spoke to The New York Times about his plans, noting that without processed food, “the world would be a very, very hungry place”.

Art of the deal The US government is striking minerals deals with companies that many experts doubt will actually be able to deliver on their promises to mine rare earths, the FT reports. A more optimistic analyst says it’s a “VC-type approach”.

news.Title: Cracks appear in US-UK security co-operation after Trump-Starmer tensions
news.AI_Summary: Differences over war in Iran are hitting working relationships between diplomats, officials and military personnel
news.Content:
The first cracks in US-UK security co-operation are appearing as tensions between Donald Trump and Sir Keir Starmer over Iran shake working relationships between diplomats, officials and military personnel.

The US president’s recent public criticism of the UK prime minister — deriding Starmer as “no Winston Churchill” and a leader who has tried to “join wars after we’ve already won” — has already had a chilling effect behind the scenes, according to people familiar with the situation.

Some of this has been a feature since Trump returned to the White House. The handful of American officials seconded to UK government departments are increasingly being asked to leave meetings when sensitive information is discussed, in contrast to a more permissive approach previously, one of the people said.

A more recent element of friction, noted by Washington, is the longer time taken by the UK to approve requests for American aircraft to use British military sites such as RAF Fairford in Gloucestershire, the person said.

In the past these requests had been “rubber-stamped” by the UK but now were “stickier” and “there’s that little bit of extra tension in the system”, the person said.

Starmer’s reluctance to allow the US to use British bases to launch its initial offensive strikes on Iran became a flashpoint in UK-US relations.

“Starmer is getting singled out among European leaders for a particular battering by Trump over Iran,” said one former senior British official. “You can see why things might get a bit tense.”

UK officials insisted there had been no change to UK security policy. They said any proposed US operation from a base in the UK is considered on a case-by-case basis, under the range of national and international treaties under which the US operates in the UK.

British officials have also had to adjust to a White House operating through a tighter inner circle under Trump during his second presidency. UK diplomats say their traditional channels in Washington have narrowed, with access increasingly dependent on a small group of advisers.

“Under this administration we see our access to senior political decision makers diminishing, because the people we have access to are having their own access curtailed,” said one person familiar with the exchanges.

The shift has fuelled unease in London that informal networks and institutional ties — a backbone of the special relationship — are proving less reliable in shaping policy or securing early insight into US decisions.

One UK official strongly disputed the characterisation, however. While all international diplomats were having to contend with Trump’s inner circle, “our embassy is one of the best placed in Washington, with links right into the centre of the system,” the person said, adding that there was a strong day-to-day working relationship between British and American officials.

Some analysts believe that the historically strong links between British and American officials are now proving less of a “guardrail” to the political tumult between the two nations during Trump’s second term in the White House.

“Chagos is a good example,” said Olivia O’Sullivan, director of the UK in the World programme at Chatham House, citing the UK government’s deal to hand sovereignty of the archipelago to Mauritius and lease back the island of Diego Garcia, home to a crucial joint UK-US military base.

“Initially the UK government was confident that even if Trump was scathing in public, the US State Department was behind the deal. Lately it’s got to the point where it is trickling down,” she said.

While officials are expected to take their cue from the political leadership, the fragmentation of the Maga movement and the confusion about Washington’s strategic goals in Iran make it difficult for American diplomats and personnel. This in turn “poses questions for UK officials”, O’Sullivan said.

Nonetheless, some senior figures remain sceptical about the idea of any deterioration in the bilateral relationship. Lord Peter Ricketts, former UK national security adviser, said operational-level co-operation between military staffs and intelligence exchange appeared to be continuing “pretty much as before”.

He cited the “iceberg principle” that most of the Anglo-American relationship functions out of sight and unaffected by surface turbulence.

While conceding that there is “clearly damage to trust” at the political level, Ricketts said daily contacts continued, Americans were still stationed in UK bases and that it was not unusual for either nation to want to hold private discussions without the other side.

While Trump has repeatedly heaped censure on Starmer, the prime minister’s national security adviser Jonathan Powell and business adviser Varun Chandra, who is formally the UK special envoy to the US on trade and investment, have strong links into the American administration.

UK foreign secretary Yvette Cooper speaks regularly to US secretary of state Marco Rubio, including most recently on the fringes of the G7 foreign ministers meeting near Paris last Friday.

The special relationship has weathered staunch differences of opinion on significant issues in the past.

Lord Simon McDonald, a former permanent secretary at the Foreign Office, highlighted that the US invasion of Grenada in 1983 and the US raid on Libya in 1986 had both been “controversial in the UK” but had not substantially affected long-term ties.

The UK and US enjoy “the deepest bilateral relationship we have with any country in the world and intelligence, security and defence are at the heart of it”, he said.

British officials are hopeful that a state visit by King Charles III to Washington at the end of April, followed by the Prince and Princess of Wales travelling to the US to attend the football World Cup this summer, may help bolster transatlantic relations, given Trump’s affection for the British royal family.

A UK government spokesperson said: “The US is our principal defence and security partner and we continue deep co-operation in the national interest. This includes the US operating from British bases as they have done for decades.”

The White House referred a request for comment to the Pentagon.

A Pentagon official said: “The US and the UK have a long-standing, strong defence relationship that has continued even through the present conflict.”

news.Title: US considers tying Nvidia and AMD AI chip exports to foreign investment pledges
news.AI_Summary: Draft rule would require countries to invest in America in exchange for advanced semiconductors
news.Content:
The US commerce department has proposed requirements that nations whose companies want to buy large volumes of Nvidia and AMD chips for AI data centres would have to commit to invest in America.

The draft rule, which could change after an inter-agency review, creates a tiered process for approving exports based on the total computing power of the chips sold to a company.

The top tier would require the home country of buyers to invest in US domestic AI infrastructure, according to people who have read the rule.

Some people familiar with the proposal said it was designed to enable the US to approve advanced chip export deals similar to arrangements that the Trump administration last year agreed with the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia.

The commerce department in November approved the export of chips to the UAE’s G42 and Saudi Arabia’s Humain in deals that will help the countries boost domestic AI data centre capacity in exchange for pledges to invest in America.

Nvidia stock fell as much as 2 per cent after Bloomberg first reported the proposed rule on Thursday, before recovering.

“Any rule that the administration issues will promote the American tech stack,” said one US official.

The commerce department said: “We successfully advanced exports through our historic Middle East agreements, and there are ongoing internal government discussions about formalising that approach.”

It also pushed back against reports that the administration was adopting a version of the AI diffusion rule unveiled in the final months of Joe Biden’s administration. That rule created a system of export controls for advanced chips that was tiered based on country risk.

“Today there was reporting that we were returning to the AI diffusion rule,” the commerce department said. “We will not. It was burdensome, over-reaching and disastrous.”

The proposed rule creates a tiered process of approvals based on the volume of chips a foreign company wants to buy.

In some cases, the company would be required to hold informal consultations with the US government before Nvidia or AMD could apply for licences to export advanced chips to those customers.

One person familiar with the situation said other agencies had been asked to respond to the commerce department proposal over the next week.

The rule would have no bearing on the export of high-end chips to US adversaries such as China, which are covered by separate export controls.

But they come as the government is still debating what conditions to attach to licences to let Nvidia sell H200 chips to China. President Donald Trump told the company’s chief executive Jensen Huang several months ago that his administration would approve licences for the exports.

As a condition for getting the green light for H200 exports, Huang agreed that Nvidia would give 25 per cent of the revenues from their sale to the US government.

Nvidia declined to comment.
